g.data-narration: Welcome to Quick Sort - one of the most efficient sorting algorithms in computer science.
Quick Sort uses divide-and-conquer: select a pivot, partition around it, recursively sort sub-arrays.
g.text: A divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that selects a pivot element and partitions
the array around it. Elements less than pivot go left, greater go right.
• Average case: O(n log n) • In-place sorting • Cache-friendly

g.data-narration: Three key concepts drive Quick Sort: choosing a pivot, partitioning the array, and recursive sorting.
The recursive algorithm splits the array at pivot position, then sorts left and right portions independently.
Lomuto scheme picks last element as pivot, maintains index for smaller elements, and swaps elements ≤ pivot forward.
g.text: Uses last element as pivot. Maintains index i for elements ≤ pivot.
if A[j] ≤ pivot: i++; swap A[i], A[j]

g.data-narration: Hoare partition uses two pointers from both ends, swapping elements on opposite sides until they cross.
g.text: More efficient than Lomuto. Two pointers move toward each other.
pivot = A[low]; i = low-1; j = high+1
do i++ while A[i] < pivot; do j-- while A[j] > pivot
if i < j: swap A[i], A[j]; else: return j

g.data-narration: Pivot selection critically affects performance. Random or median-of-three prevents worst-case on sorted inputs.
Best and average cases achieve O(n log n) when pivot divides array nearly equal. Worst case is O(n²) when unbalanced.
Walking through [10,7,8,9,1,5] with pivot 5: smaller elements swap forward, pivot places at correct position.
g.text: i=-1: Scan to 1 is less than or equal to 5 (i=0): swap 10 and 1 - array becomes [1,7,8,9,10,5]
After loop: swap A[1] and A[5] - array becomes [1,5,8,9,10,7]
Pivot at index 1. Recursively sort [1] and [8,9,10,7]

g.data-narration: Worst case O(n squared) occurs when pivot is always smallest or largest element, common with sorted arrays and poor pivot choice.
g.text: Occurs when pivot is always smallest or largest element (sorted array plus first or last pivot):
n plus n-1 plus n-2 plus ... plus 1

g.data-narration: Tail call optimization reduces space to O(log n) by processing the smaller subarray first, enabling compiler optimization.
g.text: Process smaller subarray first via tail recursion to enable compiler optimization:
while low is less than high:
if (pi-low) is less than (high-pi): QuickSort(A,low,pi-1); low = pi+1

g.data-narration: Quick Sort achieves O(n log n) average with in-place sorting, but requires careful pivot selection to avoid O(n squared) worst case.
g.text: O(n log n) average, O(n squared) worst - In-place: O(1) extra space
Pivot selection critical: random or median-of-three avoid worst-case
Lomuto simpler; Hoare more efficient - Not stable - Cache-friendly
